name: Types of Relations — Reflexive, Symmetric, Transitive
whatIsIt: A RELATION on a set links its members with ordered pairs. It can have special properties. REFLEXIVE: every element relates to itself (a R a). SYMMETRIC: if a R b then b R a. TRANSITIVE: if a R b and b R c then a R c. A relation with ALL THREE properties is an EQUIVALENCE RELATION — it sorts the set into neat groups (classes).
whyNeeded: Sorting things into groups — same age, same remainder, parallel lines, 'is equal to' — relies on equivalence relations. Recognising these properties is a Class 11 NCERT topic and a JEE Main favourite in Relations & Functions.
realLifeUses: 'Is the same age as' — an equivalence relation grouping people by age
'Is parallel to' groups lines into directions
'Has the same remainder when divided by 5' groups numbers
'Is equal to (=)' — the simplest equivalence relation

vocabulary.meaning: Every element relates to itself: a R a for all a.
If a R b then b R a.
If a R b and b R c then a R c.
A relation that is reflexive, symmetric AND transitive.

story.title: Same Team
story.text: Coach says, 'Isha, is "on the same team as" a special relation?' Isha checks: 'Everyone is on their own team — reflexive. If I'm on your team, you're on mine — symmetric. If I'm with you and you're with Ravi, then Ravi and I share a team — transitive.' Coach nods: 'All three! So it's an EQUIVALENCE relation — it splits the class into teams.'
story.extractedProblem: The relation 'is on the same team as' is reflexive, symmetric and transitive. What kind of relation is it?

grids.label: pairs (a, b): the diagonal shows reflexive a R a

visual.caption: A relation on {1,2,3} as a grid: a mark in cell (a,b) means a R b. Marks all down the diagonal ⇒ reflexive; a mirror-image pattern ⇒ symmetric.

grids.label: symmetric: a R b ⇒ b R a (mirror)
transitive: a R b, b R c ⇒ a R c

standardMethod.summary: Test each property in turn. Reflexive: does a R a hold for every a? Symmetric: does a R b force b R a? Transitive: do a R b and b R c force a R c? All three ⇒ equivalence relation.
steps: REFLEXIVE? Check that every element relates to itself.
SYMMETRIC? Check that whenever a R b, also b R a.
TRANSITIVE? Check that whenever a R b and b R c, also a R c.
If all three hold, it is an EQUIVALENCE relation.

workedExamples.problem: Is the relation '=' (is equal to) on numbers an equivalence relation?
steps: Symmetric: a = b ⇒ b = a ✓. Transitive: a = b, b = c ⇒ a = c ✓.

workedExamples.problem: Is '<' (is less than) reflexive on numbers?
steps: Reflexive needs a < a for all a.
But a < a is never true.
So NO, it is not reflexive.

commonMistakes.mistake: Confusing reflexive (a R a) with symmetric (a R b ⇒ b R a).
commonMistakes.fix: Reflexive is each element to ITSELF; symmetric is about a PAIR working BOTH ways.

commonMistakes.mistake: Calling a relation an equivalence relation without checking all three properties.
commonMistakes.fix: An equivalence relation must be reflexive AND symmetric AND transitive — all three.

easy.q: a R a for every a means the relation is…
a R b ⇒ b R a means the relation is…

medium.q: a R b and b R c ⇒ a R c means the relation is…
An equivalence relation is reflexive, symmetric and…

hintLadder: No number is less than itself.

questions.q: a R a for every a is which property?
An equivalence relation needs how many properties?
a R b ⇒ b R a is which property?
'Is parallel to' for lines (a line parallel to itself) is…

teachBackPrompt: Pick a relation from daily life ('same birthday month as', 'is a friend of', 'is taller than') and test it with a grown-up: is it reflexive, symmetric, transitive? Decide whether it is an equivalence relation and explain why or why not.

revisionCard.summary: Relation properties: REFLEXIVE (a R a), SYMMETRIC (a R b ⇒ b R a), TRANSITIVE (a R b & b R c ⇒ a R c). All three ⇒ EQUIVALENCE relation, which splits the set into classes. '=' and 'is parallel to' are equivalence relations; '<' is not (not reflexive).

meta.curriculum: CBSE Class 11 / JEE Main (types of relations)

rememberIt.hook: Reflexive (self), Symmetric (both ways), Transitive (chain) → all 3 = Equivalence
rememberIt.unpack: Reflexive ≠ symmetric. Equivalence needs all three and sorts the set into classes.

studentQuestions.q: What does an equivalence relation actually do?
studentQuestions.a: It splits a set into non-overlapping groups (equivalence classes) where everything in a group is related to everything else in it.

studentQuestions.q: Reflexive vs symmetric — how do I keep them straight?
studentQuestions.a: Reflexive is one element with ITSELF (a~a); symmetric needs TWO elements and both directions (a~b and b~a).
